summary: Russian Romantic composer (1844–1908), member of the Mighty Five, and one of the great orchestrators in Western music history. Best known for the symphonic suite Scheherazade, the Capriccio Espagnol, the Russian Easter Festival Overture, and the operatic interlude 'Flight of the Bumblebee.' His operas include Sadko, The Tale of Tsar Saltan, The Snow Maiden, and The Golden Cockerel. He taught Stravinsky and completed Mussorgsky's Night on Bald Mountain. His orchestration textbook Principles of Orchestration uses only examples from his own works.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov (1844–1908) was a Russian Romantic composer and one of the Mighty Five (with Balakirev, Mussorgsky, Borodin, and Cui). He served as Inspector of Naval Bands and professor at the Saint Petersburg Conservatory. He was celebrated as one of the greatest orchestrators of his era, publishing the treatise Principles of Orchestration (posthumous), which uses only examples from his own works. He taught Igor Stravinsky and provided Stravinsky with Russian folk melodies for The Firebird. He completed Mussorgsky's Night on Bald Mountain and helped complete Borodin's Prince Igor (with Glazunov). He attended 'Quartet Fridays' hosted by the music patron Mitrofan Belyayev. Wikipedia

works.name: Scheherazade
works.description: Rimsky-Korsakov's four-movement orchestral suite (Op. 35, 1888), inspired by the One Thousand and One Nights. The four movements are: (I) 'The Sea and Sinbad's Ship,' (II) 'The Kalendar Prince,' (III) 'The Young Prince and the Young Princess,' and (IV) 'Festival at Baghdad / The Sea.' Scheherazade herself is represented by a solo violin (with harp) playing a high sixteenth-note triplet theme; the Sultan is represented by an E-D-C-B-flat (E-D-C-A-sharp) motif in the low brass. The finale depicts a shipwreck. A 'fanfare motif' for trombones and muted trumpets is reprised in the last movement. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Scheherazade (title storyteller) represented by a solo violin with harp accompaniment, playing high sixteenth-note triplets
examples: A violin solo accompanied by harp represents the title character ... in a tone poem by this composer that begins with 'The Sea and Sinbad's Ship'
harp and a solo violin playing high sixteenth note triplets to represent the title storyteller of a suite

works.name: Capriccio Espagnol
works.description: Rimsky-Korsakov's five-movement orchestral showpiece (Op. 34, 1887) on Spanish themes. Its five movements are (I) 'Alborada,' (II) 'Variations,' (III) 'Alborada' (in different key/orchestration), (IV) 'Scena e canto gitano' (featuring five successive cadenzas for horn/trumpets, violin, flute, clarinet, and harp over a snare drum), and (V) 'Fandango asturiano' with castanets. The strings imitate guitars after the second Alborada movement. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Five-movement work including two 'Alborada' movements and a closing 'Fandango asturiano' (with castanets)
examples: A 'Fandango asturiano' and two 'Alborada' movements are part of his Capriccio Espagnol
This Russian's two Spanish Overtures ... [and] Capriccio Espagnol

works.name: Russian Easter Festival Overture
works.description: Rimsky-Korsakov's 1888 overture ('Overture on Liturgical Themes'), based on Russian Orthodox chants from the Obikhod. Opens in 5/2 time with a Lento mystico liturgical melody in the winds, followed by a violin cadenza. Features unusual time signatures (5/2, 2/1, 3/1). Ends with an Allegro agitato section with exclamations of 'Resurrexit!' A solo cello evokes the theme 'An angel cried out.' It was dedicated to Mussorgsky and Borodin. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Opens in 5/2 time; subtitled 'Overture on Liturgical Themes'; based on Russian Orthodox (Obikhod) chants
examples: The score for this piece explains that the unusual 5/2 opening tempo should be subdivided into 2 and 3 beats
This piece opens in 5/2 time and features a solo cello evoking the theme 'An angel cried out'

clues.clue: Ends with 'Resurrexit!' exclamations, shifting into 2/1 time

clues.clue: Uses liturgical chants with unusual time signatures (5/2, 3/1, 2/1) from the Obikhod
examples: This composer experimented with unusual time signatures like 3/1, 2/1, and 5/2 in a piece based on the Obikhod liturgical chants

works.name: Flight of the Bumblebee
works.description: Interlude from Act III of Rimsky-Korsakov's opera The Tale of Tsar Saltan (1900), depicting Prince Gvidon Saltanovich's transformation into an insect (bee) by the Swan-Bird. Features frantic, rapid descending chromatic runs in the flutes and first violins. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Rapid descending chromatic runs depict Prince Gvidon's transformation into an insect by the Swan-Bird in The Tale of Tsar Saltan
examples: In an interlude from this composer's opera The Tale of Tsar Saltan, frantic chromatic runs depict a buzzing insect
In a fast-paced interlude from one of his operas, a rapidly descending chromatic scale played by flutes and first violins depicts the buzzing of the metamorphosed son of the title Tsar Saltan

works.name: Sadko
works.description: Rimsky-Korsakov's 1898 opera (and earlier tone poem/musical tableau) based on a Russian bylina. Depicts the gusli-playing folk hero Sadko in Novgorod, who dives to the Sea-King's realm. Features three 'Guest Songs': the Varangian Guest Song, the Indian Guest Song ('Song of India'), and the Venetian Guest Song. The 'Song of India' was adapted by Tommy Dorsey into a jazz trombone standard. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Three 'Guest Songs': Varangian, Indian ('Song of India'), and Venetian — the 'Song of India' was adapted by Tommy Dorsey as a jazz trombone standard
examples: A seven-scene opera by this composer is partly set in the realm of the Sea-King and includes three arias depicting the Varangian, Indian, and Venetian guest
Tommy Dorsey adapted an aria by this composer into the jazz standard 'Song of India'

clues.clue: The opera was based on an earlier 'musical tableau' itself based on a bylina; depicts the gusli-playing hero Sadko
examples: This Rimsky-Korsakov opera was based on his earlier 'musical tableau' itself based on a bylina. It contains arias named for Varangian, Indian, and Venetian Guest and depicts a gusli-playing hero

works.name: The Tale of Tsar Saltan
works.description: Rimsky-Korsakov's 1900 opera, based on Pushkin's poem. The title character's son, Prince Gvidon Saltanovich, is aided by the Swan-Bird (who transforms him into insects — bee, fly, mosquito — to reach his father). Also contains the 'Flight of the Bumblebee.' Tsar Saltan's third chosen bride is picked despite the old woman Babarikha's disapproval. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Tsar Saltan's son Prince Gvidon is transformed into insects by the Swan-Bird; the third sister is chosen as bride against the old woman Babarikha's wishes
examples: In an opera by this composer, the title character chooses the third sister as his bride to the dismay of the old woman Babarikha
Rapid descending chromatic notes open a frantic passage from an opera by this composer of Sadko that occurs after the Swan-Bird changes Prince Gvidon Saltanovich into an insect

works.name: The Snow Maiden (Snegurochka)
works.description: Rimsky-Korsakov's 1882 opera. The Snow Maiden is the subject of a wooing contest between Lel and Mizgir. In a tragic ending, she declares her love for Mizgir and melts in the spring sunlight. The 'Dance of the Tumblers' is sung by the chorus after Mizgir swears to win her heart. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Snow Maiden is wooed by Lel and Mizgir; melts and dies after declaring her love for Mizgir
examples: The title character of this other Rimsky-Korsakov opera is the subject of a wooing contest between Lel and Mizgir. In a tragic ending, she melts and dies after declaring her love for Mizgir
this composer wrote an opera in which the chorus sings the 'Dance of the Tumblers' after Mizgir swears to win the heart of the title character

works.name: The Golden Cockerel
works.description: Rimsky-Korsakov's 1909 satirical opera (his last), immediately banned when published. Calls for a rare tenor altino voice for the role of the Astrologer (a eunuch). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Satirical opera, immediately banned when published; calls for a rare tenor altino voice for the role of the Astrologer (eunuch)
examples: A satirical opera by this composer calls for a rare tenor altino to perform the role of an eunuch Astrologer, and was immediately banned when published

comprehensive_summary:
Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov (1844–1908) was a Russian Romantic composer and a member of the Mighty Five (the Mighty Handful), the nationalist school that also included Mily Balakirev, Modest Mussorgsky, Alexander Borodin, and César Cui. He served as Inspector of Naval Bands and professor at the Saint Petersburg Conservatory. He was celebrated above all as an orchestrator of extraordinary skill, and his posthumously published Principles of Orchestration — which uses only examples from his own works (Capriccio Espagnol, Scheherazade, etc.) — remains a landmark text. He described the bassoon as 'an atmosphere of senile mockery.'

His most famous orchestral works are the symphonic suite Scheherazade (Op. 35, 1888) and the Capriccio Espagnol (Op. 34, 1887). In Scheherazade, the storyteller is represented by a solo violin with harp accompaniment playing high sixteenth-note triplets, while the Sultan is represented by an E-D-C-B-flat bass motif in the low brass. The suite's four movements are 'The Sea and Sinbad's Ship,' 'The Kalendar Prince,' 'The Young Prince and the Young Princess,' and 'Festival at Baghdad / Shipwreck.' His wife protested its 1910 adaptation as a ballet by Michel Fokine and Léon Bakst. The Capriccio Espagnol's five movements include two Alborada movements (identical except for key and orchestration), 'Variations,' 'Scena e canto gitano' (with five successive cadenzas over a snare drum for horn/trumpets, violin, flute, clarinet, and harp), and a closing 'Fandango asturiano' with castanets.

His Russian Easter Festival Overture ('Overture on Liturgical Themes') opens in the unusual 5/2 time with a Lento mystico liturgical melody, includes a violin cadenza, and ends with exclamations of 'Resurrexit!' shifting into 2/1 time. It is based on Obikhod chants and was dedicated to Mussorgsky and Borodin.

His operas include: Sadko (1898) — featuring three 'Guest Songs' for a Varangian, Indian (the 'Song of India,' later adapted by Tommy Dorsey as a jazz standard), and Venetian visitor; The Tale of Tsar Saltan (1900) — famous for the 'Flight of the Bumblebee' interlude depicting Prince Gvidon's transformation into an insect by the Swan-Bird; The Snow Maiden (1882) — in which Snegurochka melts after declaring her love for Mizgir; and The Golden Cockerel (1909, his last opera) — immediately banned, calling for a rare tenor altino voice as the eunuch Astrologer.

Rimsky-Korsakov taught Igor Stravinsky; Stravinsky borrowed Russian folk melodies from his collection 100 Russian Folk Songs for The Firebird. Rimsky-Korsakov completed Mussorgsky's Night on Bald Mountain (the version most commonly heard today) and helped Glazunov complete Borodin's Prince Igor. He attended 'Quartet Fridays' at the home of music patron Mitrofan Belyayev, whose circle (unlike the Five) emphasized Western craftsmanship over nationalist distinctiveness.

Tone Poem: Scheherazade represented by solo violin with harp playing high sixteenth-note triplets; the Sultan by an E-D-C-B-flat bass motif; four movements including 'The Sea and Sinbad's Ship' and 'Festival at Baghdad'
Tone Poem: his wife protested its 1910 adaptation as a ballet by Michel Fokine and Léon Bakst

Tone Poem: five movements including two 'Alborada' sections (identical except for key and orchestration), 'Scena e canto gitano' (five cadenzas over snare drum), and 'Fandango asturiano' with castanets
Overture: opens in 5/2 time with a Lento mystico liturgical melody; based on Obikhod chants; ends with 'Resurrexit!' exclamations shifting into 2/1 time
Opera: rapid descending chromatic runs in the flutes depict Prince Gvidon's transformation into an insect by the Swan-Bird
